Artificial Intelligence

Machine Learning

Deep Learning

Quantization

LLM

4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Make your AI models smaller and faster — without retraining them.
Imagine you own a coffee shop.
Your coffee recipe never changes. Every barista follows the same instructions every day. Since the recipe is fixed, you print it on a small laminated card instead of carrying around a giant recipe book.
Now imagine customers walking in.
One wants an espresso.
Another wants a caramel latte with oat milk.
A third orders an iced mocha with extra foam.
You can’t predict every order in advance. Instead, you quickly adapt to each customer as they arrive.
That’s exactly how dynamic quantization works.
The recipe is like the model’s weights — they stay the same, so we compress them ahead of time.
The customer orders are like the activations — they’re different every time, so we process them on the fly.

What is Dynamic Quantization?
Dynamic quantization is a technique where:

• Model weights are converted to a lower precision format (such as INT8) before deployment.

• Activations remain in FP32 until inference.

• During inference, activations are temporarily quantized based on the current input.
Think of it as packing your suitcase before a trip but deciding what jacket to wear only after checking today’s weather.

What Happens During Inference?
Consider a simple neural network:
Input
↓
Linear Layer
↓
OutputBefore Quantization
Weights → FP32
Input → FP32
Matrix Multiply → FP32
Output → FP32Everything uses 32-bit floating-point numbers.
Accurate? Yes.
Fast? Not always.
Memory efficient? Definitely not.

After Dynamic Quantization
Weights → INT8 ✅
Input → FP32
↓
Activation is temporarily quantized
↓
INT8 Matrix Multiplication
↓
Output converted back to FP32 (if needed)Notice something interesting?
Only the weights stay permanently compressed.
The activations are compressed only while the model is running, then converted back if the next layer requires floating-point values.

Why Is It Called Dynamic?
Because the model doesn’t assume every input looks the same.
Imagine two customers.
Customer 1
[-0.5, 0.2, 0.9]These values are relatively small.
Now another customer arrives.
Customer 2
[-10, 5, 20]Completely different!
Instead of forcing both customers into the same “one-size-fits-all” scale, dynamic quantization recalculates the scale and zero point for each input (or batch).
It’s like using different-sized coffee cups depending on the customer’s order instead of serving everything in a giant bucket.

Why Not Quantize Everything Ahead of Time?
You might wonder:
“If quantization is so useful, why not quantize activations beforehand too?”
Because activations don’t exist until someone provides an input.
Think about it.
Your model can process:

• Cat images 🐱

• Medical scans 🩻

• Financial transactions 💳

• Customer reviews ⭐
Each input produces different activation values.
Trying to pre-quantize activations would be like trying to prepare tomorrow’s customer orders before the shop even opens.

Advantages
✅ Extremely easy to apply to an already trained model.
✅ No retraining required.
✅ No calibration dataset required.
✅ Significantly reduces model size.
✅ Often speeds up CPU inference for Transformer and LSTM models.
It’s one of those rare optimizations where you get noticeable improvements with just a few lines of code.

Disadvantages
Like every engineering trade-off, dynamic quantization isn’t perfect.
❌ Activations still need to be quantized during execution, adding a small runtime cost.
❌ GPUs usually benefit less than CPUs.
❌ Some operations continue running in floating-point precision.
If you need maximum speed, static quantization often performs better — but requires more setup.

Dynamic vs. Static Quantization
Dynamic Quantization 🥊 vs. Static Quantization
🏆 Weights
Tie 🤝
Both quantize the model weights before deployment.

🏆 Activations
Dynamic: Quantized while the model is running.
Static: Quantized before deployment using calibration data.

🏆 Calibration Dataset
Dynamic: ❌ No
Static: ✅ Yes

🏆 Ease of Use
Dynamic: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
Static: ⭐⭐⭐

🏆 CPU Speed
Dynamic: Good
Static: Better

Winner?
Choose Dynamic Quantization when you want the simplest optimization with minimal effort.
Choose Static Quantization when squeezing out every bit of CPU performance is worth the extra setup.
Think of it like meal prep.
Dynamic quantization cooks the ingredients ahead of time but prepares the final dish when the customer orders.
Static quantization prepares almost everything before the restaurant even opens.
More preparation, but faster serving.

PyTorch Example
import torch
from torch.quantization import quantize_dynamicquantized_model = quantize_dynamic(
model,
{torch.nn.Linear},
dtype=torch.qint8
)That’s it.
With just a few lines of code, a trained model can often become much smaller and noticeably faster on CPUs — without retraining.

Key Takeaway
Dynamic quantization is one of the simplest ways to optimize an AI model for inference.
By storing weights in INT8 and quantizing activations only when new inputs arrive, it strikes a practical balance between speed, memory usage, and implementation effort.
If static quantization is like preparing every meal before the restaurant opens, dynamic quantization is having a master chef who can cook quickly based on whatever customers order.
And sometimes, that’s exactly the flexibility you need.

In one sentence:
Dynamic quantization stores a model’s weights in a lower-precision format while quantizing activations at runtime, making inference faster and models smaller without requiring retraining or a calibration dataset.
